text: We are just looking at the bladder neck size. Is it okay or you would like to reconstruct that or what is your take on this? I think we will need to reconstruct it but I think we will need to reconstruct it because we had too many stitches posteriorly and too close to each other. Had we placed the stitches a little bit further, we might not need to reconstruct it. We will see. Okay. So sometimes we use a six and a nine o'clock position, six inch and a nine inch. We didn't use that because I thought the bladder neck would be smaller, and it probably would have been, but we came too close in the posterior side. Right. So, always the concern about uretic orifice, really do you make any attempt to look at it or you just say, well, take it as... So, the concern is more concerned than a real issue. You are... Looking at the ureteric orifices when you incise the bladder neck.

And this stitch is an RB one. So it is such a small stitch that if you haven't come close to the UOs when you cut the bladder neck, you're not going to come close to the UO doing the anastomosis. If you have them close to the UOs, when you're cutting the bladder neck, as you will in a patient with lateral lobes, then I like to reconstruct the bladder neck laterally, fish-mouth it, so that the ureteric orifices will be recessed and be free from the anastomosis. Other people, Dr. Bhandari, and I'd ask Ethan what he would do, They would not. They would reconstruct it, not reconstruct it laterally, just do the anastomosis the way it is. And when they end, they'll just put another stitch, which you might see what you're doing here. Yeah, I mean it looks like you'll have to do an anterior tennis racket. I'll tell you that I've changed my ways a little bit in that I almost always reconstruct and I make a really very small bladder neck, probably twenty-eight, thirty French opening at greatest dimension. Has not shown to make any functional outcome difference. But yeah, I almost always reconstruct even small bladder necks like this one and make it really very small.

I asked would you to do the anastomosis because the way we run it is we run two rooms. By this time, I'm starting the next room. And so I am a little rusty about the anastomosis. I need to do about five or ten to get up to speed. So I probably would have placed the stitches further in the posterior aspect and I would not have reconstructed this but this is the way Vuju does it and you know there's no difference in outcomes as Ketan mentioned. It's looking beautiful really. Now, as I see on the other side, you have a grasper, right? Pro-grass, fenestrated. It's a fenestrated grasper. Now, at one point of time, one of the mentors told us that we should use two needle holders. What is your take on that, really? It's better to use two needle holders. It's more natural to use two needle holders. I thought we would have to use them because the pelvis is narrow. But this is cost-saving. I mean, we've used this grasper for the dissection. We've used it for the bipolar. And so, you know, that's two hundred bucks less or ten thousand rupees less. Twenty thousand rupees less. So certainly a cost-cutting measure, I guess, on table.

But it is tougher because the feel is different. You use more force holding the needle holder. You have to use less force for the grasper. With the needle holder, you can sew with both hands. With the grasper, you can sew with both hands, but you've got to be very precise in how you hold it. So, Vuj is doing a fantastic job. I don't think that he needed the second needle holder and he just saved the hospital and the patient twenty thousand rupees. Yes, of course. Thank you very much for that really. The one point which I wanted to ask you was, One of the mentors did show us while you are coming into the bladder neck it is possible that at the posterior or towards the capsule of the bladder you enter and at mucosa you enter at a different level because of the curve. Would that happen sometime?

The surgeon incises the anterior bladder wall sharply to assess its full thickness, ensuring the incision is perpendicular to the posterior bladder wall. The hook instrument is used with a wristed motion to maintain a right angle to the posterior dorsal wall.
